speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape five, an interview with Robert Ness on August the 24th, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is tape number five, an interview with Robert Ness on August the 24th, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then from Vilna, you were taken on a transport to Estonia along with 6,000 other Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately. I don't know whether how big the transport was, but I think about the total taken. It was not only from Vilna, they took from Kaunas too, from Kovne. I didn't know about it, but we met them later on in Estonia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Estonia, you came first to a slave labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was right, it was labor camps, slave labor camp. And my first thought as we passed, was I thought we go from there to Paneriai, which is six kilometers. And suddenly the train is moving and moving and moving--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean you thought you were going to be killed? You were thought to be massacred?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. I was absolutely convinced. As I told you, when I wrote that note to my father, I was absolutely convinced that's it. Paneriai, that what's I thought only. But pretty soon, the train left on the Rose Cemetery. That was where Pilsudski's heart was buried, his mother was buried there. And suddenly, I take a look, Dyneburg. I knew enough of the map that it was Vincent, and now we're in Latvia. Then we arrived in Riga on the railroad station. And we go and run to get water, and I was jumped out, managed to run to the water to bring in to the boxcars.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it looks like we're going to Estonia after all. And besides, the guards, we suddenly hear [NON-ENGLISH]. We'd never heard that language, it was Estonians. [NON-ENGLISH] means devil, it's their regular like son of a bitch or whatever it is. It's just kurat, K-U-R-A-T.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I saw they were Estonian too because I saw the flag. [NON-ENGLISH], the blue, black, white on the armbands. But we used to call them the kurats because that's all we heard was kurat, kurat. And we came to Estonia, and they unloaded us in Ereda. Now later on, Ereda became a bigger camp. It became up to here, but we were still down there in the swamps. I had boots, nice ones still-- good ones, leather ones-- and we were there in the camp, and we went to work. Now I went on the road, and I worked on the on the road to [NON-ENGLISH] going through. Keep in mind, there was oil fields and-- not oil field but those kinds of plates that had oil content, and they used to press them out to unroll the mines. And I met a guard, a nice guy, a Dutchman. He was working for the [NON-ENGLISH], that was the [NON-ENGLISH] or the construction--

speaker: interviewer
text: Engineers.

speaker: interviewee
text: --engineers, and so forth. So they used to bring us from the camp, and we used to work for them. And kind of befriended with him. And he gave me, I found out, Mussolini was overthrown. As I told you, I was all ears to find out what goes on in the world. That he was overthrown and the king is now--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I got some German newspapers from him, I got a [NON-ENGLISH], which was the main organ of the Nazi party, so I could see-- I mean, I've learned to read between the lines a long time ago, so I could see what goes on in the world, which-- and so I kind of-- I said, you know what? Let's go to the farmer, I have something. So I made arrangements. I gave the farmer the watch, and not realizing-- you know, and the earrings-- that you're not a boss yourself if you're common and so forth. You brought this watch and earrings from Vilna ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Vilna. When they gave us a loaf of bread, I opened up the loaf of bread, and I put it in and closed it. And they made a search, but it was already dark. And I was in another corner. And low and behold, just about four or five people before me, they stopped the search. They had to close it, otherwise, I would have maybe gotten shot altogether, OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had the loaf of bread, and I had my two earrings and a watch. So I make an arrangement with that particular Estonian farmer who speaks Russian, that he should feed him and me, the Dutch guy and me, and he can have it. And I gave it to him. Oh, now I go and had lunch. You should see a dinner, it was with potatoes, was meat, was-- oh!

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't last long because what happened was we having appel, which is, you know, getting together of all the prisoners. And there is this lagerfuhrer comes up, and somebody's trying to push his nephew on him as a [NON-ENGLISH], a runner, and he didn't like the choice. And suddenly, I was standing, as I told you, in the small boy. I mean, I wasn't small, but I was still small among the people involved, so I was in the first line.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm in the first line of the lanes involved, and suddenly that lagerfuhrer, the commander of the camp, he winks with his finger towards my direction, to come out and to come to him. And I still don't know whether that's me. And he again, this way. And finally it looks like he's-- so I show with my finger me? Yeah, yeah, yeah. I come out, and he asked me speak the Dutch? I say yeah. He said, OK, [NON-ENGLISH]. And the other boy did not get the job of [NON-ENGLISH], being a runner. And I was thinking to myself, son of a gun. Here I have my good food, and I get my newspapers and the news, and now I've lost it. Of course, it was a blessing in disguise. What the hell, under oath, how long would you have been there on the throne? Another few days, you would have been away from the farmer, could have never gone back there. To another work detail.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, but at that time, I figured here, I mean, I lose my connection, and the newspapers and the food. And-- oh! But [NON-ENGLISH] was [NON-ENGLISH], so I became a [NON-ENGLISH]. And I still had stamps with me too. Believe it or not, I had those, and I still had some stamps. Now I became [NON-ENGLISH], I give him my stamps, I hand to the lagerfuhrer. And I was there almost probably another few months--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Ereda?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ereda. They were still building it. It was slummed. It's really bad there. And then suddenly, he takes about 150 people, plus his own administration, so to speak. So after all, it's a [NON-ENGLISH] among them.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the head of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's a staff master sergeant, he was the head of this particular camp, yes. An SS man-- it's an [NON-ENGLISH], so he was oberscharfuhrer. And he takes and we go to Narva in an open gondola. And it's January of '44, cold, freezing cold. Maybe December of '44. Just open, but it wasn't completely open, it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: December '43 or December--

speaker: interviewee
text: December '44, January 1944. I came September-- say maybe November, December 1943 or beginning of 1944, OK? I'm already 14 years old. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's right, I'm 14 years old. And we go to Narva. We stay in Narva a night over. And then they take us by riverboat, Narva River going south to the Peipus Lake. In Russian, it's [NON-ENGLISH]. And we get off there and we come to Soski Agusalu, a camp just being built, and I was there the runner. Runner, you know, when he used to go and counting the people. I used to follow through, get me this one, go me-- get some sort. But there is one instance I have to tell about it because the guards suddenly became Estonians. In Ereda, they were already Estonians. And the story in Ereda I wanted to go back for just a minute, the first few days, we go out to get water. So I go with, figure I'll wash up. And suddenly, I forgot the soap. I had a piece of soap by that time they gave us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So instead of going through the main gate and maybe to go in there, I figure, eh, I'll go through the wire. My barrack is right there, ask somebody, go, there I had a piece of soap. And there is an Estonian guard going, and he sees me kind of like running through the camp. And suddenly, I'm just about I'd say-- I don't know Estonian. I learned some Estonian counting-- so I show him that I want something there. And he said [NON-ENGLISH]. in Estonian. Later I found what it means. What is going on here? Who is that? And I try German, I see he doesn't understand German.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said kurat, takes his rifle, loads it up like that. Bang! I could hear the whistle right next to my ear. He missed me. I wasn't further from him than 8, 10 feet, at most.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I started to run away from him. So I started to run, but I was still alert not to run straight. I was running in a zigzag. Running like that, running. That's right, running like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was running in the zigzag, and I hear another shot. And the bullet hits between my legs, and the sand hits me in the face. And I got away, and I came to the people there with the water. I didn't know what would happen if I go back, whether they were going to recognize me or not. I came back into the camp, and everything was quiet. Then I went to work, I told you, on the road. And was getting some good food, maybe became friends with a Dutch guard.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you became the runner.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I became the runner. So now I don't have my earrings with the diamonds, and I don't have the watch anymore. I had a few good dinners, no question about it. Real good, solid dinners.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Soski Agusalu?

speaker: interviewee
text: So then we took us by Narva the way to Soski Agusalu. It must have been until the beginning of February.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you then, eventually, went back to Ereda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, by way of Kuremaa.

speaker: interviewer
text: First Kuremaa--

speaker: interviewee
text: First Kuremaa, and then back to Ereda. That was all walking march. There is a story that I have to tell you, and I'll make it quick, because it's so hilarious, it's unreal. This particular lagerfuhrer was a such a jerk. He was such a braggart.

speaker: interviewer
text: This master sergeant?

speaker: interviewee
text: The master sergeant, the oberscharfuhrer. So he started this, oh, I can do this and I can do that. And the Jews in there who used to play like wow, [NON-ENGLISH]. So that idiot says he can go ahead and take a bath, take a steam bath, and then run into the snow, and then go back and take a steam bath, and then go back into the snow. And everybody [NON-ENGLISH]. And he did it all right, got pneumonia too. And we lost the lagerfuhrer, and he happens to be good one in the nature. He was a nut, but he was reasonable enough. He wasn't beating anybody. So those guys were pulling him, and baiting him, and he wound up with a good pneumonia. And we lost our lagerfuhrer. He got pneumonia.

words.text: and he wound up with a good pneumonia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they sent somebody else, and he was a real no good. He was from the [NON-ENGLISH], which is private first class. His specialty was to go with a stick, and go in the women's barracks to check their vaginas, whether they were clean. That's what he used to do, that son of a bitch.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he used to go there, and that was his day, spending looking for the girls. And he used to come with-- God forbid, being alone, he use to come, and have a few men with him, and so on, to watch the cleanliness of the girls' vaginas. That bastard. And then he was gone and--

speaker: interviewer
text: He was in Soski--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Soski Agusalu. But he was just a very short period of time, maybe a week. But I created commotions there once. I went to the village of Soski Agusalu. And at that time, we got Russian guards. Russian guards, not the Estonians anymore. The [NON-ENGLISH] or Estonian Russians who were ethnic Russians but were part of the free Estonia between the wars. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was a translator. Suddenly, the [NON-ENGLISH] needed somebody to translate to get the farmers come with horse in places. So I go there, and I wanted to get a newspaper. So the newspaper didn't come yet, so I had a Russian guard-- an Estonian Russian-- and he wanted to go dancing. I said I've got to go back to the camp. The end result is I go dancing with him, and stay out till God knows what hour, was probably 12 o'clock. And we march back-- now mind you, this is now winter, it's night. Nights, you can see, you could read the paper at nighttime because it's all white nights. And we walk, we come back.

words.text: And we walk, we come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we come back and the Russian lieutenant, he starts [NON-ENGLISH]. It means you bastard, you. You Jew swine, or something like that. And suddenly my lagerfuhrer calls out [NON-ENGLISH]. I say [NON-ENGLISH], lagerfuhrer. [NON-ENGLISH], where were you? So I tell him, I say, listen, I was in the camp to arrange for organization, taught farmers. I knew I was in trouble, I say. And I was coming to the camp myself without a guard. I say, I couldn't go to the camp by myself, so I waited for the guard. And he was going dancing, and I just had to wait for him.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came into the camp. I found out there was suddenly an appel, and suddenly, Nussbaum is missing. So the whole camp is standing outside, and they looked, and right, and left, and under the bed, and Nussbaum wasn't there. So all of them thought I ran away because I was going constantly outside of the camp, and this or that. And he says, OK, you can't go out of the camp any more. He said, you are staying in. He was nice, really. Another one would have knocked the shit out of me, maybe killed me altogether. And this way, we came to Kuremaa.

words.text: can't go out of the camp any more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eventually, though, you were in Kuremaa for a while--

speaker: interviewee
text: For a while, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --then back to Ereda--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ereda, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and then to Stutthof.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. From Ereda to Lagedi. We stayed in Lagedi for a while. That was before Tallinn. They dumped us. There was no boat, no ship to take us. So they took us to Lagedi, dumped us on the railroad tracks there, across from the railroad station. And we stayed there for about a few. But there is another thing I have. Oh, Donnie, this is really for the books. This lagerfuhrer in Ereda didn't give a damn what goes on. Absolutely didn't give a damn, so the Jews were running the camp. The Jewish elder there, he was making arrangement with food from Estonians. I mean, it was unreal. You wouldn't believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the first time you were in Ereda?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm talking Kuremaa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kuremaa, sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kuremaa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what the hell was I wanted to tell about it? [GROANS] son of a gun.

speaker: interviewer
text: So suddenly, they sent an [NON-ENGLISH]. Supposedly a nurse, male nurse. He came, he gave injections to kill people, and they didn't die so quick enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: He took them one by one, going onto a stump-- like to a big stump from a tree-- and he had a plain, simple pick that you pick the ground and break ice. And he killed just about 20 people, one of them was an elderly people, with a pick. And his name was [NON-ENGLISH], and he was from Danzig. I don't know whether he was ever caught after the war or not, but that was an idea of a nurse, that was-- to be a nurse. So that was in Kuremaa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, eventually--

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually we came to-- oh! Lagedi. I have to tell you that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this particular lagerfuhrer was showing off with his new boy he found. The youngster knows German, and he was showing off before a guy who was a scrivener in his office. You see, I found one who speaks German, and he's not a professor. His German is just as good as yours. So he kind of treated me nicely. So then we come to Lagedi, and everybody knew that he's a good guy, a crazy one that doesn't bother anybody. So suddenly, he's taking about 200 people that is supposedly are going with him. Well, everybody in his presence runs to get into the line to go with him, after all. I don't remember his name. He was a scharfuhrer, corporal. So why, of course, I among them. I run into one line. He takes a look at the line, and he sees me there. [NON-ENGLISH]! I figured, you son of a bitch. I was your [NON-ENGLISH], your runner. What are you throwing me out from the group? So I go back into the line. He catches me again. [NON-ENGLISH]! I squeeze in a third time. He catches me again. [NON-ENGLISH]! Well, [NON-ENGLISH] is [NON-ENGLISH]. What can I do? You see, this were almost the strongman. It was they didn't know probably when it's coming or not, what all kills. So that son of a gun knew that it's going to be killed, so he figured somehow or other, took me out. Can you imagine that? Three [NON-ENGLISH]. What kind of [NON-ENGLISH]? A Nazi [NON-ENGLISH] if you ever heard, was [NON-ENGLISH]! But you thought--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, of course. I thought like all the others. Stay in the line, let's go with him. After all, he is the honey.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he really-- he saved you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's no question about. There's no question about.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now we come from Lagedi to Tallinn, and then we go by ship to Stutthof. We arrive in Stutthof after about three or four day travel, I don't know. And then we walk from Danzig, from the wharf, from the harbor in Danzig, by foot to Stutthof. Stutthof was located in the area that was before the war the free city of Danzig. It was, I mean, the surrounding part of Danzig, free state. And this was the summer of 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: August. Now I'm talking August 1944. And we come to Stutthof. Suddenly, we see a huge camp with lamps, with lights, with dogs. I mean, I really never saw that one like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we come to camp. And as we're walking into the camp, believe it or not, a fellow, suddenly somebody is talking. [NON-ENGLISH]. A Polish guy, is there a Nussbaum among you? And somebody answered him back, [NON-ENGLISH], yes. [NON-ENGLISH], the old one or the young one? He says the young one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, who was it but Mr. Zukowski, a neighbor of ours from Raczki, before the war. A man who was all the dealings, merchants dealings between merchants, with my father. I remembered him distinctly. He used to have honey, honey bees, so I used-- Anyway, so I talk to him. He asked me about what happened to father. I don't know where father is. I know mother was killed, Paula is killed. He ask about Aunt Sarah. I told him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said to me-- and we had to go back for disinfection in Stutthof the next morning. So he says you know? We have Naroszewicz here. He's a young priest who was in Raczki. He knew the family too, and he knew Paula from school. So he said, I'll talk to him. Maybe you can see what he can do for you. There is this block has six priests, and maybe he can arrange something for you. I said talk to him. I don't know. He works in the disinfections or someplace. I will tell him about it. And I'll be a son of a gun if Mr. Naroszewicz doesn't show up next day at the gates, at the wires of the disinfection place. And suddenly people look at me, look at that! He just arrived, somebody's asking for you. I was already like a big shot, you know? Suddenly, old inmates-- where would I go from old inmates?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I talked to him, and he asked about the family. And then I came back into Stutthof, and Zukowski told me talk to him again, I'll talk to him. And he fixed me up with as an orderly in block six. Six had about six to nine priests, and then they had a [NON-ENGLISH] who was sick, so I was attending to him. You know, attending, it's really a joke. I used to put the pot that you eat from, put it under his behind. And he used to know, he used to put it in, wash it a little bit, and put it together with the other stuff, and that was it. But what happened was the [NON-ENGLISH] were-- I told you in history, they were claiming that they had a kingdom and Danzig was their headquarters-- in the Middle Ages I'm talking now-- and that the Poles were Polandizing them and the Germans were Germanizing them. And he used to buttonhole anybody who wanted to listen, especially if somebody who was before with the League of Nations or somebody, a politician or-- oh. He used to buttonhole them. But be that as it may, on Sunday, I used to come to the block where I used to work. I didn't sleep there, didn't live there, but I used to come and work there.

speaker: interviewer
text: As an orderly?

speaker: interviewee
text: As an orderly. But on Sunday, I used to come and write letters for the [NON-ENGLISH], had to be in German. Many of them didn't know German, so I used to write a few cards. Suddenly, in a camp, you send out mail. Who ever heard of it? Well, you see, for the Jewish prisoner, it was one thing for inmates. But the Poles and the others, they were-- Danes used to get packages, help the to Jewish boys in Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Danes and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Danes were there. Yeah, there were some political prisoner Danes that they kept them in Stutthof, but they used to get packages from Denmark, from the Red Cross. Always sardines, Swiss chocolate, I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: And they shared with the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to share with the Jewish boy. Just about every Dane had his Jewish boy. Absolutely. The younger, for sure. If you got older, they used to kind of send you off and go for the younger kids. But the point was so I got the job. So I already-- Zukowski used to give me some food. I used to earn some soup and some bread. I wasn't going hungry. And an important part was, which I wouldn't be around again, there was suddenly a big search for Jewish boys. And they collected about 500, and they took them to Auschwitz. Well, my [NON-ENGLISH]. He quick, quick, quick got a hold of me, and suddenly, I go on the third floor. On the beds was three rows.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he opens up a roof in the ceiling plate, puts me in there. And after it was all over, and the quota was made and it was quiet, I came out. How about that one? Otherwise, I would have been in Auschwitz with those 500 and would've been a goner.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, the breaks that you have to get. When one thing is unconnected to the other, it's just disconnected events. Disconnected one from the other like the pearls. Disconnected. And each one you have to survive just by sheer luck. And cunning too, of course. In my case, by my wits, but just the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I survived this way in Stutthof. And then there was the march form Stutthof, around January 1945 I'm talking now. Now they were evacuating the camp or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Evacuating the camp, that's right. The Russians started coming closer. Be that as it may, the camp is being evacuated. Now I was already thinking that I will run away, absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I organize myself already. I brought myself pants, which had red crosses. But just little ones like the kapos or the big shots were wearing. Cost me, gave them some bread or something. Because I figured--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I started to scratch it off, scratch it off. By the time the march started, there were no crosses on my pants. You really have to look like the bastard was looking to see the shades. And that was the same on these pants, And on the coat. I got a coat, which had a very tiny, little fancy X. I erased it slowly, slowly. By the time we were marching, there was no more X on the coat. But my jacket, inside the coat, that had a big red cross, and I couldn't get rid of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I just carried it. But I got sick, I got sick of typhoid. And I couldn't walk. My legs were swollen. And I took off the shoes, and I was pushed the finger and it made a hole in the skin in the leg. That was from beside no nourishment, you say he's got just grips snow. But the [NON-ENGLISH] used to disappear constantly. And you said it was typhus. Typhus?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. It was all green. I used to-- later on when I ran away and I was hiding among the farmers, I was sick. It was all green. I used to-- and I had to-- and they kind of nourished me back to a little bit of health.

words.text: I used to-- and I had to--

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was when-- so you escaped from this death march?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but the way I escape. Before escaping, I couldn't walk anymore, so I finally sat at the end on a marker. You know, a marker on a stone you have? And so I sat there and suddenly, they call on it's end. That's how I saw how they're killing the people who fall behind. And I said [NON-ENGLISH]. Blockfuhrer comes up, and he knew me because I was working in six. He said [NON-ENGLISH]. I say, [NON-ENGLISH], I can't go anymore. And suddenly the guard loads the rifle, and loads it up and puts it up just like that. And he's from a distance like you from me. And believe it or not, I got up from that stone, and I started to move. And I came in the village in the first row. It couldn't have been strength, it was just-- I don't know what it was. But I knew I have to run away because I'll never make it. From the church, next day, I run away with two other Polish boys. And out I was of Stutthof.
